translation.content: We know what you're thinking: why did it take so long to get Binh on the RA Podcast? There's no easy answer, especially given that he's been a favourite of ours for over a decade now. So to make up for lost time, the revered digger lands on the series with a tantalising three-hour mix, recorded live at Nowadays in New York earlier this year. Crammed with rare and unreleased gems spanning house, techno and electro, RA.989 is heavy on drama, catching the Düsseldorf native in unbridled performance mode. This is Binh in 2025. But rewind across the past two decades and you'll find a DJ who excels in pretty much any situation, whether warming up for Zip or Margaret Dygas, going back-to-back with masters like Nicolas Lutz or rolling out solo sets that stretch across ten or even 12 hours. He's acted out all these scenarios most often at Berlin institution Club der Visionaere, the cosy canalside club that was previously a kind of spiritual home. (He'd sometimes spend every night of the week there, before parenting duties got the better of him.) To play for half a day you need a lot of records, and the backbone of Binh's craft has long been a freakish passion for vinyl. Along with Lutz, Vera and others in CDV's orbit, he approaches the pursuit of wax, new and old, with next-level dedication and a fondness for blind buys. As Matt Unicomb wrote in 2015, his enviable collection informs his DJing in one simple but inspiring way: he plays incredible music you can't hear anywhere else. That's true of RA.989, although among the un-Shazamables are many tracks that are either out or coming out on Binh's popular Time Passages label. The mix finds him on a house-y tip, luxuriating in roomy basslines, driving 303s and twinkly melodies—with just the right amount of evil. There are countless moments. For whatever reason, he was clearly feeling it that Sunday morning. Lock in and savour a one-of-a-kind DJ, completely in the zone. – Carlos Hawthorn What have you been up to recently? Besides the usual touring and gigs, on May 3rd I had a Time Passages X party here in Berlin, and I honestly couldn't be happier with how it went! Playing gigs has always been a part of my life, but since last year, with the ten-year anniversary tour, things have become more serious around the parties we organise as well. Of course my main focus is still on DJing and on my music and I'm really happy to see Time Passages growing naturally in a meaningful direction. I'm also very grateful for all the new music and artists I've been able to connect with lately. And of course, my family always comes first—they're the core of everything. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? The mix is a live recording from the last time I played at Nowadays in New York on March 2nd. I used a classic setup: two Technics SL-1210 M5G turntables and an Allen & Heath Xone:96 mixer. The set was part of the club's incredible 24-hour non-stop party, specifically during the Sustain-Release takeover. The event started on Saturday evening, and I played on Sunday from 4 PM to 7 PM. There wasn't a specific concept behind the set—I just followed the energy of the moment and let things unfold naturally, responding to the vibe of the crowd. The mix features many upcoming and already released tracks on Time Passages, as well as a lot of new music currently circulating out there. What are you looking forward to in the near future? I'm really looking forward to what's coming next—new projects, new music and deeper connections. I already have a lot of releases planned on Time Passages, and I can't wait to finally share them all. There's also Time Passages X, a special limited-edition series, obviously vinyl-only, just one release per year. I truly believe in constant evolution, both in music and in life—staying true to yourself while always growing. And of course, I'm also looking forward to taking a holiday with my family. It's always important to take a break, recharge and find new inspiration. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? Well, the world is a bit mad right now—there are so many things that need to be fixed. But since becoming a father, I've become especially sensitive to issues affecting children. Children are the future, yet we're clearly not doing enough to protect or care for them. The reality is often much worse than what we see, and it's painful to witness. We need to do better—for their sake and for the generations to come. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? That's always a tough one for me, because there have been so many clubs that shaped different phases of my journey—and they've all been important in their own way. I'm lucky that I still get surprised and inspired today, and I hope that never changes. You're a famously deep digger. How have you seen digging culture change in recent years? Do you dig mostly online or still in record shops? Haha, I wouldn't really call myself a deep digger these days. I don't spend as much time digging as I used to. Back in the day, I'd spend hours flipping through crates everywhere I could. Now, I know what I'm looking for and where to find it, so it happens more naturally. Sometimes records just find their way to me. Digging, for me, isn't something to hype—it's just natural. It's like being a cyclist: you love riding, so you ride. Same with digging. It's not about making a statement, it's just part of how I connect with music. I still really enjoy going to record shops, being present in that space. That physical experience is special. I also feel really happy and lucky to live in Berlin, where there are still so many record shops—big and small, new stuff, old stuff—it's amazing. It keeps that culture alive, and I love it. At the same time, I buy a lot of records online too. It's convenient and sometimes the only way to find certain pieces. So I guess it's a mix of both worlds now. And it's important for me to stay up to date with what's coming out. There's so much new good music around. Keeping my ears open keeps things exciting and constantly evolving. The Berlin scene is constantly shifting. What continues to inspire you musically about the city? And is there anything you think the scene could do better? Shifting is always a good thing—it's part of evolution. Genres blend, boundaries move and what comes next is often something new and fresh. That constant change is what keeps things interesting. In Berlin, I'm inspired by how diverse and open the music scene is. There's space for so many different sounds, and people are usually open-minded. You can feel the freedom to experiment and that gives energy to both artists and dancers. Of course, like any scene, there's always room to grow. Sometimes I wish there was even more support for pushing boundaries—taking risks rather than sticking to what works. But overall, I feel lucky to be part of a city that's so alive musically.
translation.blurb: Three hours of high drama, recorded live at Nowadays in New York.
